content: I think I need to use a sysfs to display device info. I found the implementation of debugfs and procfs in pull request list and issue list. However it seems that sysfs abstraction didn't implemente to the rust kernel. I'm not sure whether I missed somthing or I just have to call sysfs C APIs by bindgen?
I don't know of any abstractions that exist for that. Maybe @Andreas Hindborg or @Ariel Miculas know of something
If they don't exist then you will need to add sysfs.h to the header file that bindgen reads, then create an abstraction over those interfaces
Are you working on something that might wind up in-tree?

Trevor Gross said:

Are you working on something that might wind up in-tree?

Temporarily not. I'm just doing device development now.
If you're just experimenting then you can use the bindings directly, but it probably makes more sense to write abstractions if you are working on something more long term
If you do write them, feel free to share :)
I don't think sysfs has been covered by anyone yet

Trevor Gross said:

If you do write them, feel free to share :)

All right, I think it could be a long term work :)

Andreas Hindborg said:

I don't think sysfs has been covered by anyone yet

I've got a pair of bindings, but I haven't touched them in a while, so you can consider this as up for grabs. https://github.com/YakoYakoYokuYoku/linux/tree/sysfs-support

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I don't think sysfs has been covered by anyone yet

I've got a pair of bindings, but I haven't touched them in a while, so you can consider this as up for grabs. https://github.com/YakoYakoYokuYoku/linux/tree/sysfs-support

Good job mate! Thanks a lot!

Maybe this is helpful: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20241208131545.386897-1-daniel@sedlak.dev/
Just found it while looking for kobject in mailing list.

Can anyone help me figure out how sysfs abstractions should look? Or more specifically if something similar already exists in current tree (so I can model things after it)?

I specifically want to use device sysfs, so I don't think it makes sense to have abstraction for kobject and stuff.
You can check out the configfs abstractions
You never use a "raw" device sysfs file, you should always use the bus-specific type. That is where the sysfs bindings should be added, NOT to the driver core itself.

Greg KH said:

You never use a "raw" device sysfs file, you should always use the bus-specific type. That is where the sysfs bindings should be added, NOT to the driver core itself.

Is there any documentation regarding bus-specific sysfs entiries. Or is it just the bus drivers section in the sysfs page?
Or maybe just a kernel header file? I am specifically intrested in sysfs for a device using a platform driver.
Oh, I think maybe you meant this: https://www.linuxfoundation.org/blog/blog/how-to-create-a-sysfs-file-correctly ?

That's a copy of my 2013 blog post about this topic which is here http://kroah.com/log/blog/2013/06/26/how-to-create-a-sysfs-file-correctly/

That's kind of close, you shouldn't be wanting to create a "raw" sysfs abstraction, the api should be going through the bus, as it is the bus-type that your driver is dealing with, and is handling the sysfs files for.

The only part of the kernel that should be dealing with "raw" sysfs files right now is filesystems, and for them, someone really needs to just take the time and write a proper abstraction so that they don't have to do that. We never expected filesystems to want to touch sysfs when it was created, so we kind of just ignored that portion of the kernel :)

Greg KH said:

That's a copy of my 2013 blog post about this topic which is here http://kroah.com/log/blog/2013/06/26/how-to-create-a-sysfs-file-correctly/

That's kind of close, you shouldn't be wanting to create a "raw" sysfs abstraction, the api should be going through the bus, as it is the bus-type that your driver is dealing with, and is handling the sysfs files for.

Does "raw" sysfs abstraction also includes abstractions for device_attribute and driver_attribute?
I am using gpio-aggregator driver as reference for how platform driver should probably create sysfs entries.

driver_attribute should hopefully never be needed, the in-kernel use of it is very small and should really be revisited again as it's almost always a bad idea (options/attributes should be on a per-device level, not a per-driver level)

device_attribute is needed, of course, but again, that ties into the platform_driver api, and overall the driver core apis, that is not a "raw" sysfs interaction (i.e. with a struct kobject).

good luck!
